The amount of the heat required to raise the temperature of the same gas through the same temperature range at constant volume is (gas constant R = 2 c a l m o l - 1 - K - 1 )

questions.html: 70 cal of heat is required to raise the temperature of 2 moles of an ideal gas from 30℃ to 35℃ while the pressure of the gas is kept constant. questions.questionHtml: 70 cal of heat is required to raise the temperature of 2 moles of an ideal gas from 30℃ to 35℃ while the pressure of the gas is kept constant. The amount of the heat required to raise the temperature of the same gas through the same temperature range at constant volume is (gas constant R=2 cal mol-1-K-1)
questions.solutionText: Solution Specific heat at constant pressure ( C p ) is the amount of heat Q required to raise n moles of substance by ∆ θ when pressure is kept constant. Then C p = Q n ∆ θ Given, Q =70 cal, n = 2 , ∆ θ = 35 - 35 ℃ = 5 ℃ ∴ C p = 70 2 × 5 =7 cal m o l - 1 - K - 1 From Mayer's formula C p - C V = R where R i s g a s c o n s t a n t ( = 2 c a l m o l - 1 ) ∴ 7 - C V = 2 ⇒ C V = 5 c a l m o l - 1 - K - 1 Hence, amount of heat required at constant volume ( C V ) is Q ′ = n C V ∆ θ Q ′ = 2 × 5 × 5 = 50 c a l
questions.solution: Solution Specific heat at constant pressure ( C p ) is the amount of heat Q required to raise n moles of substance by ∆ θ when pressure is kept constant. Then C p = Q n ∆ θ Given, Q =70 cal, n = 2 , ∆ θ = 35 - 35 ℃ = 5 ℃ ∴ C p = 70 2 × 5 =7 cal m o l - 1 - K - 1 From Mayer's formula C p - C V = R where R i s g a s c o n s t a n t ( = 2 c a l m o l - 1 ) ∴ 7 - C V = 2 ⇒ C V = 5 c a l m o l - 1 - K - 1 Hence, amount of heat required at constant volume ( C V ) is Q ′ = n C V ∆ θ Q ′ = 2 × 5 × 5 = 50 c a l
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Specific heat at constant pressure (Cp) is the amount of heat Q required to raise n moles of substance by ∆θ when pressure is kept constant. Then
Cp=Qn∆θ
Given, Q=70 cal, n=2,
∆θ=35-35℃=5℃
∴ Cp=702×5=7 cal mol-1 -K-1
From Mayer's formula Cp-CV=R
where R is gas constant (=2 cal mol-1)
∴ 7-CV=2
⇒ CV=5 cal mol-1-K-1
Hence, amount of heat required at constant volume (CV) is
Q′=nCV∆θ
Q′=2×5×5=50 cal

questions.solutionText: Solution y 1 = n 1 λ 1 D d for bright fringes y 2 = n 2 λ 2 D d for bright fringes To coincide n 1 λ 1 = n 2 λ 2 n 1 × 650 = n 2 × 520 ∴ n 1 n 2 = 4 5 For minimum value of n 1 and n 2 n 1 = 4 n 2 = 5 y 1 m i n = 4 × 650 × n m × 150 m 0.5 × 10 - 3 m = 7800 × 10 - 6 m = 7.8 m m
questions.solution: Solution y 1 = n 1 λ 1 D d for bright fringes y 2 = n 2 λ 2 D d for bright fringes To coincide n 1 λ 1 = n 2 λ 2 n 1 × 650 = n 2 × 520 ∴ n 1 n 2 = 4 5 For minimum value of n 1 and n 2 n 1 = 4 n 2 = 5 y 1 m i n = 4 × 650 × n m × 150 m 0.5 × 10 - 3 m = 7800 × 10 - 6 m = 7.8 m m
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
y1=n1λ1Dd for bright fringes
y2=n2λ2Dd for bright fringes
To coincide
n1λ1=n2λ2
n1×650=n2×520
∴ n1n2=45
For minimum value of n1 and n2
n1=4 n2=5
y1min=4×650×nm×150m0.5×10-3m
=7800×10-6m
=7.8 mm

questions.html: An alternating emf is applied across a parallel combination of a resistance R, capacitance C and an inductance L. If IR, IL, IC are the currents through R, L and C, respectively, then the diagram which correctly represents the phase relationship among IR, IL, IC and source emf E is given by
questions.questionHtml: An alternating emf is applied across a parallel combination of a resistance R, capacitance C and an inductance L. If IR, IL, IC are the currents through R, L and C, respectively, then the diagram which correctly represents the phase relationship among IR, IL, IC and source emf E is given by
questions.solutionText: Solution In a parallel RLC circuit, voltage E is common across all elements. Phase relationships of currents w.r.t. E : I R : in phase with E I L : lags E by 90 ° (points downward if E is along x-axis) I C : leads E by 90 ° (points upward if E is along x-axis) So the phasor diagram must show: E and I R along the same direction (horizontal) I C upward ( + 90 ° ) I L downward ( − 90 ° ) Option (3) shows exactly this: I C upward, I R and E horizontal, I L downward. Answer: Option (3)
questions.solution: Solution In a parallel RLC circuit, voltage E is common across all elements. Phase relationships of currents w.r.t. E : I R : in phase with E I L : lags E by 90 ° (points downward if E is along x-axis) I C : leads E by 90 ° (points upward if E is along x-axis) So the phasor diagram must show: E and I R along the same direction (horizontal) I C upward ( + 90 ° ) I L downward ( − 90 ° ) Option (3) shows exactly this: I C upward, I R and E horizontal, I L downward. Answer: Option (3)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
In a parallel RLC circuit, voltage E is common across all elements.

Phase relationships of currents w.r.t. E:
IR: in phase with E
IL: lags E by 90° (points downward if E is along x-axis)
IC: leads E by 90° (points upward if E is along x-axis)

So the phasor diagram must show:
E and IR along the same direction (horizontal)
IC upward (+90°)
IL downward (−90°)

Option (3) shows exactly this: IC upward, IR and E horizontal, IL downward.

Answer: Option (3)

questions.questionHtml: Three blocks of masses m1, m2 and m3 are connected to a massless string on a frictionless table as shown in the figure. They are pulled with a force of 40 N. If m1=10 kg, m2=6 kg and m3=4 kg, then tension T2 will be
questions.solutionText: Solution Since, the table is frictionless, i.e., it is smooth, therefore, force on the block is given by F = m 1 + m 2 + m 3 a ⇒ a = F m 1 + m 2 + m 3 ⇒ a = 40 10 + 6 + 4 = 40 20 = 2 m s - 2 Now, the tension between 10 kg and 6 kg masses is given by T 2 = m 2 + m 3 a = 4 + 6 2 = 10 × 2 T 2 = 20 N
questions.solution: Solution Since, the table is frictionless, i.e., it is smooth, therefore, force on the block is given by F = m 1 + m 2 + m 3 a ⇒ a = F m 1 + m 2 + m 3 ⇒ a = 40 10 + 6 + 4 = 40 20 = 2 m s - 2 Now, the tension between 10 kg and 6 kg masses is given by T 2 = m 2 + m 3 a = 4 + 6 2 = 10 × 2 T 2 = 20 N
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Since, the table is frictionless, i.e., it is smooth, therefore, force on the block is given by
F=m1+m2+m3a
⇒ a=Fm1+m2+m3
⇒ a=4010+6+4=4020=2 m s-2
Now, the tension between 10 kg and 6 kg masses is given by
T2=m2+m3a
=4+62=10×2
T2=20 N

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: An Earth satellite orbiting close to the surface of the Earth takes about 90 minutes per revolution. A satellite orbiting close to the surface of the Moon also takes about 90 minutes per revolution. Which of the following is true?
questions.questionText: An Earth satellite orbiting close to the surface of the Earth takes about 90 minutes per revolution. A satellite orbiting close to the surface of the Moon also takes about 90 minutes per revolution. Which of the following is true?
questions.question: An Earth satellite orbiting close to the surface of the Earth takes about 90 minutes per revolution. A satellite orbiting close to the surface of the Moon also takes about 90 minutes per revolution. Which of the following is true?
questions.prompt: An Earth satellite orbiting close to the surface of the Earth takes about 90 minutes per revolution. A satellite orbiting close to the surface of the Moon also takes about 90 minutes per revolution. Which of the following is true?
questions.html:
An Earth satellite orbiting close to the surface of the Earth takes about 90 minutes per revolution. A satellite orbiting close to the surface of the Moon also takes about 90 minutes per revolution.

Which of the following is true?
questions.questionHtml:
An Earth satellite orbiting close to the surface of the Earth takes about 90 minutes per revolution. A satellite orbiting close to the surface of the Moon also takes about 90 minutes per revolution.

Which of the following is true?
questions.solutionText: Solution For a satellite orbiting close to the surface of a planet of radius R and density ρ : T = 2 π R g = 2 π R 2 G M / R = 2 π R 3 G M Since M = 4 3 π R 3 ρ : T = 2 π R 3 G ⋅ 4 3 π R 3 ρ = 2 π 3 4 π G ρ = 3 π G ρ T depends only on the density ρ , not on the size of the planet. Since both satellites have the same time period T = 90 min: ρ m = ρ e Answer: (3) ρ m = ρ e
questions.solution: Solution For a satellite orbiting close to the surface of a planet of radius R and density ρ : T = 2 π R g = 2 π R 2 G M / R = 2 π R 3 G M Since M = 4 3 π R 3 ρ : T = 2 π R 3 G ⋅ 4 3 π R 3 ρ = 2 π 3 4 π G ρ = 3 π G ρ T depends only on the density ρ , not on the size of the planet. Since both satellites have the same time period T = 90 min: ρ m = ρ e Answer: (3) ρ m = ρ e
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

For a satellite orbiting close to the surface of a planet of radius R and density ρ:

T=2πRg=2πR2GM/R=2πR3GM

Since M=43πR3ρ:

T=2πR3G⋅43πR3ρ=2π34πGρ=3πGρ

T depends only on the density ρ, not on the size of the planet.

Since both satellites have the same time period T=90 min:
ρm=ρe

Answer: (3) ρm=ρe

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The energy band diagrams for three semiconductor samples of silicon are as shown. We can then assert that
questions.questionText: The energy band diagrams for three semiconductor samples of silicon are as shown. We can then assert that
questions.question: The energy band diagrams for three semiconductor samples of silicon are as shown. We can then assert that
questions.prompt: The energy band diagrams for three semiconductor samples of silicon are as shown. We can then assert that
questions.html: The energy band diagrams for three semiconductor samples of silicon are as shown. We can then assert that
questions.questionHtml: The energy band diagrams for three semiconductor samples of silicon are as shown. We can then assert that
questions.solutionText: Solution Interpreting the energy band diagrams: X : No dotted line in the band gap ⇒ no impurity energy level ⇒ undoped (intrinsic). Y : Dotted line closer to the conduction band ⇒ donor level ⇒ doped with pentavalent (5th group) impurity ⇒ n-type. Z : Dotted line closer to the valence band ⇒ acceptor level ⇒ doped with trivalent (3rd group) impurity ⇒ p-type. Answer: Option (4) — X is undoped, Y is doped with pentavalent impurity, Z is doped with trivalent impurity.
questions.solution: Solution Interpreting the energy band diagrams: X : No dotted line in the band gap ⇒ no impurity energy level ⇒ undoped (intrinsic). Y : Dotted line closer to the conduction band ⇒ donor level ⇒ doped with pentavalent (5th group) impurity ⇒ n-type. Z : Dotted line closer to the valence band ⇒ acceptor level ⇒ doped with trivalent (3rd group) impurity ⇒ p-type. Answer: Option (4) — X is undoped, Y is doped with pentavalent impurity, Z is doped with trivalent impurity.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Interpreting the energy band diagrams:

X: No dotted line in the band gap ⇒ no impurity energy level ⇒ undoped (intrinsic).

Y: Dotted line closer to the conduction band ⇒ donor level ⇒ doped with pentavalent (5th group) impurity ⇒ n-type.

Z: Dotted line closer to the valence band ⇒ acceptor level ⇒ doped with trivalent (3rd group) impurity ⇒ p-type.

Answer: Option (4) — X is undoped, Y is doped with pentavalent impurity, Z is doped with trivalent impurity.

questions.text: The electric field of a plane electromagnetic wave is given by E → = E 0 i ^ + j ^ 2 cos ⁡ k z + ω t . At t = 0 , a positively charged particle is at the point x , y , z = 0 , 0 , π k . If its instantaneous velocity at t = 0 is v 0 k ^ , the force acting on it due to the wave is:

questions.html: The electric field of a plane electromagnetic wave is given by E→=E0i^+j^2cos⁡kz+ωt . At t=0 , a positively charged particle is at the point x,y,z=0,0,πk . If its instantaneous velocity at t=0 is v0k^ , the force acting on it due to the wave is:
questions.questionHtml: The electric field of a plane electromagnetic wave is given by E→=E0i^+j^2cos⁡kz+ωt . At t=0 , a positively charged particle is at the point x,y,z=0,0,πk . If its instantaneous velocity at t=0 is v0k^ , the force acting on it due to the wave is:
questions.solutionText: Solution The wave travels in − z direction (since k z + ω t implies − k ^ direction). At t = 0 , position ( 0 , 0 , π k ) : cos ⁡ ( k ⋅ π k + 0 ) = cos ⁡ π = − 1 E → = − E 0 i ^ + j ^ 2 Wave travels in − k ^ direction, so: B → = k ^ p r o p × E → c = ( − k ^ ) × ( − E 0 i ^ + j ^ 2 ) c = E 0 c 2 ( k ^ × ( i ^ + j ^ ) ) = E 0 c 2 ( j ^ − i ^ ) Velocity of particle: v → = v 0 k ^ Force on positive charge: F → = q ( E → + v → × B → ) v → × B → = v 0 k ^ × E 0 c 2 ( j ^ − i ^ ) = v 0 E 0 c 2 ( k ^ × j ^ − k ^ × i ^ ) = v 0 E 0 c 2 ( − i ^ − j ^ ) F → = q [ − E 0 i ^ + j ^ 2 + v 0 E 0 c 2 ( − i ^ − j ^ ) ] = q ⋅ − E 0 2 ( 1 + v 0 c ) ( i ^ + j ^ ) Since q > 0 and the coefficient is negative, F → is antiparallel to i ^ + j ^ 2 . Answer: Option (3)
questions.solution: Solution The wave travels in − z direction (since k z + ω t implies − k ^ direction). At t = 0 , position ( 0 , 0 , π k ) : cos ⁡ ( k ⋅ π k + 0 ) = cos ⁡ π = − 1 E → = − E 0 i ^ + j ^ 2 Wave travels in − k ^ direction, so: B → = k ^ p r o p × E → c = ( − k ^ ) × ( − E 0 i ^ + j ^ 2 ) c = E 0 c 2 ( k ^ × ( i ^ + j ^ ) ) = E 0 c 2 ( j ^ − i ^ ) Velocity of particle: v → = v 0 k ^ Force on positive charge: F → = q ( E → + v → × B → ) v → × B → = v 0 k ^ × E 0 c 2 ( j ^ − i ^ ) = v 0 E 0 c 2 ( k ^ × j ^ − k ^ × i ^ ) = v 0 E 0 c 2 ( − i ^ − j ^ ) F → = q [ − E 0 i ^ + j ^ 2 + v 0 E 0 c 2 ( − i ^ − j ^ ) ] = q ⋅ − E 0 2 ( 1 + v 0 c ) ( i ^ + j ^ ) Since q > 0 and the coefficient is negative, F → is antiparallel to i ^ + j ^ 2 . Answer: Option (3)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The wave travels in −z direction (since kz+ωt implies −k^ direction).

At t=0, position (0,0,πk):
cos⁡(k⋅πk+0)=cos⁡π=−1

E→=−E0i^+j^2

Wave travels in −k^ direction, so:
B→=k^prop×E→c=(−k^)×(−E0i^+j^2)c=E0c2(k^×(i^+j^))=E0c2(j^−i^)

Velocity of particle: v→=v0k^

Force on positive charge:
F→=q(E→+v→×B→)

v→×B→=v0k^×E0c2(j^−i^)=v0E0c2(k^×j^−k^×i^)=v0E0c2(−i^−j^)

F→=q[−E0i^+j^2+v0E0c2(−i^−j^)]=q⋅−E02(1+v0c)(i^+j^)

Since q>0 and the coefficient is negative, F→ is antiparallel to i^+j^2.

Answer: Option (3)

questions.text: Mass is non-uniformly distributed over a rod of length l . Its linear mass density varies with distance x from one end according to λ = k x 2 (where k is a constant). Find the position of the centre of mass from the lighter end of the rod.

questions.solutionText: Solution x c m = ∫ 0 l x d m ∫ 0 l d m = ∫ 0 l x ⋅ λ d x ∫ 0 l λ d x = ∫ 0 l x ⋅ k x 2 d x ∫ 0 l k x 2 d x = ∫ 0 l x 3 d x ∫ 0 l x 2 d x = l 4 / 4 l 3 / 3 = 3 l 4 Answer: (3) 3 l 4

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
xcm=∫0lxdm∫0ldm=∫0lx⋅λdx∫0lλdx=∫0lx⋅kx2dx∫0lkx2dx=∫0lx3dx∫0lx2dx

=l4/4l3/3=3l4

Answer: (3) 3l4

questions.question: A metallic rod breaks when strain produced is 0 . 2 % . Young's modulus of the material of the rod is 7 × 10 9 N m - 2 . What should be its area of cross-section to support a load of 10 4 N ?
questions.prompt: A metallic rod breaks when strain produced is 0 . 2 % . Young's modulus of the material of the rod is 7 × 10 9 N m - 2 . What should be its area of cross-section to support a load of 10 4 N ?
questions.html: A metallic rod breaks when strain produced is 0.2%. Young's modulus of the material of the rod is 7×109 N m-2. What should be its area of cross-section to support a load of 104 N?
questions.questionHtml: A metallic rod breaks when strain produced is 0.2%. Young's modulus of the material of the rod is 7×109 N m-2. What should be its area of cross-section to support a load of 104 N?
questions.solutionText: Solution Given, maximum strain = 0 . 2 % = 0 . 2 100 , Young's modulus, Y = 7 × 10 9 Nm - 2 , load = 10 4 N . In elastic limit, stress is directly proportional to strain. The proportionality constant is called Young's modulus of elasticity Y . stress = Y × strain . . . 1 Put, stress = load A , where A is area of cross-section. load A = Y × strain A = load Y × strain Put the given values, A = 10 4 7 × 10 9 × 0 . 2 100 A = 7 . 1 × 10 - 4 m 2 .
questions.solution: Solution Given, maximum strain = 0 . 2 % = 0 . 2 100 , Young's modulus, Y = 7 × 10 9 Nm - 2 , load = 10 4 N . In elastic limit, stress is directly proportional to strain. The proportionality constant is called Young's modulus of elasticity Y . stress = Y × strain . . . 1 Put, stress = load A , where A is area of cross-section. load A = Y × strain A = load Y × strain Put the given values, A = 10 4 7 × 10 9 × 0 . 2 100 A = 7 . 1 × 10 - 4 m 2 .
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

Given, maximum strain=0.2%=0.2100

, Young's modulus, Y=7×109 Nm-2,

load=104 N.

In elastic limit, stress is directly proportional to strain. The proportionality constant is called Young's modulus of elasticity Y.
stress=Y×strain ...1

Put, stress=loadA,

where A is area of cross-section.

loadA=Y×strain
A=loadY×strain

Put the given values,

A=1047×109×0.2100

A=7.1×10-4 m2.

questions.questionHtml: The centre of mass of three particles of masses 1 kg,2 kg and 3 kg is at (2,2,2). The position of the fourth mass of 4 kg to be placed in the system as that the new centre of mass is at (0,0,0) is.
questions.solutionText: Solution m 1 = 1 kg , m 2 = 2 kg , m 3 = 3 kg Position of centre of mass ( 2 , 2 , 2 ) m 4 = 4 kg New position of centre of mass ( 0 , 0 , 0 ) . For initial position, X CM = m 1 x 1 + m 2 x 2 + m 3 x 3 m 1 + m 2 + m 3 2 = m 1 × x 1 + m 2 x 2 + m 3 x 3 1 + 2 + 3 m 1 x 1 + m 2 x 2 + m 3 x 3 = 12 Similarly, m 1 y 1 + m 2 y 2 + m 3 y 3 = 12 m 1 z 1 + m 2 z 2 + m 3 z 3 = 12 and m 1 z 1 + m 2 z 2 + m 3 z 3 = 12 For new position, X CM ′ = m 1 x 1 + m 2 x 2 + m 3 x 3 + m 4 x 4 m 1 + m 2 + m 3 + m 4 0 = 12 + 4 + x 4 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 4 x 4 = − 12 x 4 = − 3 Similarly, y 4 = − 3 z 4 = − 3 ∴ Position of fourth mass ( − 3 , − 3 , − 3 ) . ^
questions.solution: Solution m 1 = 1 kg , m 2 = 2 kg , m 3 = 3 kg Position of centre of mass ( 2 , 2 , 2 ) m 4 = 4 kg New position of centre of mass ( 0 , 0 , 0 ) . For initial position, X CM = m 1 x 1 + m 2 x 2 + m 3 x 3 m 1 + m 2 + m 3 2 = m 1 × x 1 + m 2 x 2 + m 3 x 3 1 + 2 + 3 m 1 x 1 + m 2 x 2 + m 3 x 3 = 12 Similarly, m 1 y 1 + m 2 y 2 + m 3 y 3 = 12 m 1 z 1 + m 2 z 2 + m 3 z 3 = 12 and m 1 z 1 + m 2 z 2 + m 3 z 3 = 12 For new position, X CM ′ = m 1 x 1 + m 2 x 2 + m 3 x 3 + m 4 x 4 m 1 + m 2 + m 3 + m 4 0 = 12 + 4 + x 4 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 4 x 4 = − 12 x 4 = − 3 Similarly, y 4 = − 3 z 4 = − 3 ∴ Position of fourth mass ( − 3 , − 3 , − 3 ) . ^
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
m1=1 kg,m2=2 kg,m3=3 kg
Position of centre of mass (2,2,2)
m4=4 kg
New position of centre of mass (0,0,0).
For initial position,
XCM=m1x1+m2x2+m3x3m1+m2+m32=m1×x1+m2x2+m3x31+2+3m1x1+m2x2+m3x3=12
Similarly,
m1y1+m2y2+m3y3=12m1z1+m2z2+m3z3=12
and
m1z1+m2z2+m3z3=12
For new position,
XCM′=m1x1+m2x2+m3x3+m4x4m1+m2+m3+m40=12+4+x41+2+3+44x4=−12x4=−3
Similarly, y4=−3
z4=−3
∴ Position of fourth mass (−3,−3,−3).
^

questions.html: What would be maximum wavelength for Brackett series of hydrogen-spectrum?
questions.questionHtml: What would be maximum wavelength for Brackett series of hydrogen-spectrum?
questions.solutionText: Solution For Brackett series, n 1 = 4 , n 2 = 5 , 6 , 7 … … … 1 λ = R ( 1 n 1 2 − 1 n 2 2 ) where R = 1.09678 × 10 7 m − 1 , called Rydberg's constant. 1 λ = R ( 1 4 2 − 1 n 2 2 ) For maximum wavelength, n 2 = 5 1 λ max = 1.09687 × 10 7 ( 1 4 2 − 1 5 2 ) λ max = 40519 Å `
questions.solution: Solution For Brackett series, n 1 = 4 , n 2 = 5 , 6 , 7 … … … 1 λ = R ( 1 n 1 2 − 1 n 2 2 ) where R = 1.09678 × 10 7 m − 1 , called Rydberg's constant. 1 λ = R ( 1 4 2 − 1 n 2 2 ) For maximum wavelength, n 2 = 5 1 λ max = 1.09687 × 10 7 ( 1 4 2 − 1 5 2 ) λ max = 40519 Å `
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
For Brackett series, n1=4,n2=5,6,7……… 1λ=R(1n12−1n22)
where R=1.09678×107 m−1, called Rydberg's constant.
1λ=R(142−1n22)
For maximum wavelength, n2=5
1λmax=1.09687×107(142−152)λmax=40519Å
`

questions.solutionText: Solution 5% of total current I passes through galvanometer, so I g = 0.05 I and I s = 0.95 I . Voltage across both is equal: I g ⋅ G = I s ⋅ S 0.05 I ⋅ G = 0.95 I ⋅ S S = 0.05 0.95 G = 1 19 G = G 19 Answer: Option (2) G 19 ^
questions.solution: Solution 5% of total current I passes through galvanometer, so I g = 0.05 I and I s = 0.95 I . Voltage across both is equal: I g ⋅ G = I s ⋅ S 0.05 I ⋅ G = 0.95 I ⋅ S S = 0.05 0.95 G = 1 19 G = G 19 Answer: Option (2) G 19 ^
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
5% of total current I passes through galvanometer, so Ig=0.05I and Is=0.95I.

Voltage across both is equal:
Ig⋅G=Is⋅S

0.05I⋅G=0.95I⋅S

S=0.050.95G=119G=G19

Answer: Option (2) G19
^

questions.solutionText: Solution Given, the figure shows the relation between speed and time. So, the area of the figure will be displacement. questions.solutionText: Solution For a cylinder rolling without slipping with a rope pulled horizontally: m = 4 kg, R = 0.1 m, F = 20 N For solid cylinder: I = 1 2 m R 2 Translational: F − f = m a ... (1) Rotational about contact point... or use torque about contact point: Torque about contact point = F ⋅ 2 R = I c o n t a c t ⋅ α I c o n t a c t = 1 2 m R 2 + m R 2 = 3 2 m R 2 F ⋅ 2 R = 3 2 m R 2 ⋅ a R 2 F = 3 2 m a a = 4 F 3 m = 4 × 20 3 × 4 = 80 12 = 6.67 ≈ 6.7 ms − 2 Answer: Option (1) 6.7 ms − 2 *
questions.solution: Solution For a cylinder rolling without slipping with a rope pulled horizontally: m = 4 kg, R = 0.1 m, F = 20 N For solid cylinder: I = 1 2 m R 2 Translational: F − f = m a ... (1) Rotational about contact point... or use torque about contact point: Torque about contact point = F ⋅ 2 R = I c o n t a c t ⋅ α I c o n t a c t = 1 2 m R 2 + m R 2 = 3 2 m R 2 F ⋅ 2 R = 3 2 m R 2 ⋅ a R 2 F = 3 2 m a a = 4 F 3 m = 4 × 20 3 × 4 = 80 12 = 6.67 ≈ 6.7 ms − 2 Answer: Option (1) 6.7 ms − 2 *
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
For a cylinder rolling without slipping with a rope pulled horizontally:

m=4 kg, R=0.1 m, F=20 N

For solid cylinder: I=12mR2

Translational: F−f=ma ... (1)
Rotational about contact point... or use torque about contact point:

Torque about contact point =F⋅2R=Icontact⋅α
Icontact=12mR2+mR2=32mR2

F⋅2R=32mR2⋅aR

2F=32ma

a=4F3m=4×203×4=8012=6.67≈6.7 ms−2

Answer: Option (1) 6.7 ms−2
*

questions.solutionText: Solution Fringe shift = ( μ − 1 ) t ⋅ D d , which is proportional to ( μ − 1 ) . For plate 1: μ 1 = 1.5 , shift = x ∝ ( 1.5 − 1 ) = 0.5 For plate 2: shift = 3 2 x ∝ ( μ 2 − 1 ) μ 2 − 1 0.5 = 3 / 2 ⋅ x x = 3 2 μ 2 − 1 = 0.75 ⇒ μ 2 = 1.75 Answer: Option (1) 1.75 !
questions.solution: Solution Fringe shift = ( μ − 1 ) t ⋅ D d , which is proportional to ( μ − 1 ) . For plate 1: μ 1 = 1.5 , shift = x ∝ ( 1.5 − 1 ) = 0.5 For plate 2: shift = 3 2 x ∝ ( μ 2 − 1 ) μ 2 − 1 0.5 = 3 / 2 ⋅ x x = 3 2 μ 2 − 1 = 0.75 ⇒ μ 2 = 1.75 Answer: Option (1) 1.75 !
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Fringe shift =(μ−1)t⋅Dd, which is proportional to (μ−1).

For plate 1: μ1=1.5, shift =x∝(1.5−1)=0.5

For plate 2: shift =32x∝(μ2−1)

μ2−10.5=3/2⋅xx=32

μ2−1=0.75⇒μ2=1.75

Answer: Option (1) 1.75
!

questions.questionHtml: Two blocks of masses 3m and m are connected by a light inextensible string passing over a smooth, fixed, and massless pulley. The system is held stationary and then released from rest. What is the acceleration of the center of mass of the two-block system?
questions.solutionText: Solution Atwood machine with masses 3 m and m : Acceleration of system: a = ( 3 m − m ) g 3 m + m = 2 m g 4 m = g 2 3 m moves down with a = g 2 , m moves up with a = g 2 Acceleration of centre of mass: a c m = 3 m ⋅ ( g 2 ) + m ⋅ ( − g 2 ) 3 m + m (taking downward as positive) a c m = 3 m g 2 − m g 2 4 m = m g 4 m = g 4 downwards Answer: (B) g 4 downwards ^
questions.solution: Solution Atwood machine with masses 3 m and m : Acceleration of system: a = ( 3 m − m ) g 3 m + m = 2 m g 4 m = g 2 3 m moves down with a = g 2 , m moves up with a = g 2 Acceleration of centre of mass: a c m = 3 m ⋅ ( g 2 ) + m ⋅ ( − g 2 ) 3 m + m (taking downward as positive) a c m = 3 m g 2 − m g 2 4 m = m g 4 m = g 4 downwards Answer: (B) g 4 downwards ^
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Atwood machine with masses 3m and m:

Acceleration of system: a=(3m−m)g3m+m=2mg4m=g2

3m moves down with a=g2, m moves up with a=g2

Acceleration of centre of mass:
acm=3m⋅(g2)+m⋅(−g2)3m+m

(taking downward as positive)

acm=3mg2−mg24m=mg4m=g4 downwards

Answer: (B) g4 downwards
^

( 2 ) from equations (1) and (2), we get, ⇒ v 1 = u 3 , v 2 = u 3 ( K.E. ) in = 2 ( 1 2 m u 2 ) = m g l ( K.E. ) final = 2 ( 1 2 m u 2 9 ) = m u 2 9 Let height attained by A after collision is l ′ ⇒ mgl ′ = mu 2 9 ⇒ l ′ = 1 9

questions.solutionText: Solution Range formula: R = u 2 sin ⁡ 2 θ g For same range with same u : sin ⁡ 2 θ 1 = sin ⁡ 2 θ 2 This holds when 2 θ 2 = 180 ° − 2 θ 1 , i.e. θ 2 = 90 ° − θ 1 θ 2 = 90 ° − 30 ° = 60 ° Answer: C. 60 °
questions.solution: Solution Range formula: R = u 2 sin ⁡ 2 θ g For same range with same u : sin ⁡ 2 θ 1 = sin ⁡ 2 θ 2 This holds when 2 θ 2 = 180 ° − 2 θ 1 , i.e. θ 2 = 90 ° − θ 1 θ 2 = 90 ° − 30 ° = 60 ° Answer: C. 60 °
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Range formula: R=u2sin⁡2θg

For same range with same u: sin⁡2θ1=sin⁡2θ2

This holds when 2θ2=180°−2θ1, i.e. θ2=90°−θ1

θ2=90°−30°=60°

Answer: C. 60°

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A parallel plate capacitor made of circular plates is being charged such that the surface charge density on its plates is increasing at a constant rate with time. The magnetic field arising due to displacement current is:
questions.questionText: A parallel plate capacitor made of circular plates is being charged such that the surface charge density on its plates is increasing at a constant rate with time. The magnetic field arising due to displacement current is:
questions.question: A parallel plate capacitor made of circular plates is being charged such that the surface charge density on its plates is increasing at a constant rate with time. The magnetic field arising due to displacement current is:
questions.prompt: A parallel plate capacitor made of circular plates is being charged such that the surface charge density on its plates is increasing at a constant rate with time. The magnetic field arising due to displacement current is:
questions.html: A parallel plate capacitor made of circular plates is being charged such that the surface charge density on its plates is increasing at a constant rate with time. The magnetic field arising due to displacement current is:
questions.questionHtml: A parallel plate capacitor made of circular plates is being charged such that the surface charge density on its plates is increasing at a constant rate with time. The magnetic field arising due to displacement current is:
questions.solutionText: Solution The displacement current density J d = ϵ 0 d E d t is uniform between the plates (since σ increases at constant rate ⇒ E increases at constant rate). Applying Ampere's law with a circular loop of radius r : B ⋅ 2 π r = μ 0 J d ⋅ π r 2 ⇒ B = μ 0 J d r 2 (inside, r < R ) B increases with r inside the plates. At r = R (periphery/cylindrical surface): B is maximum. Outside ( r > R ): B = μ 0 J d R 2 2 r , which decreases. So B is non-zero everywhere (inside and outside) and is maximum at r = R , i.e., at the imaginary cylindrical surface connecting the peripheries. Answer: Option (3)
questions.solution: Solution The displacement current density J d = ϵ 0 d E d t is uniform between the plates (since σ increases at constant rate ⇒ E increases at constant rate). Applying Ampere's law with a circular loop of radius r : B ⋅ 2 π r = μ 0 J d ⋅ π r 2 ⇒ B = μ 0 J d r 2 (inside, r < R ) B increases with r inside the plates. At r = R (periphery/cylindrical surface): B is maximum. Outside ( r > R ): B = μ 0 J d R 2 2 r , which decreases. So B is non-zero everywhere (inside and outside) and is maximum at r = R , i.e., at the imaginary cylindrical surface connecting the peripheries. Answer: Option (3)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The displacement current density Jd=ϵ0dEdt is uniform between the plates (since σ increases at constant rate ⇒E increases at constant rate).

Applying Ampere's law with a circular loop of radius r:

B⋅2πr=μ0Jd⋅πr2⇒B=μ0Jdr2 (inside, r<R)

B increases with r inside the plates.

At r=R (periphery/cylindrical surface): B is maximum.

Outside (r>R): B=μ0JdR22r, which decreases.

So B is non-zero everywhere (inside and outside) and is maximum at r=R, i.e., at the imaginary cylindrical surface connecting the peripheries.

Answer: Option (3)

questions.text: Free surface of a liquid behaves like a stretched membrane and tends to assume smallest possible area due to
questions.questionText: Free surface of a liquid behaves like a stretched membrane and tends to assume smallest possible area due to
questions.question: Free surface of a liquid behaves like a stretched membrane and tends to assume smallest possible area due to
questions.prompt: Free surface of a liquid behaves like a stretched membrane and tends to assume smallest possible area due to
questions.html: Free surface of a liquid behaves like a stretched membrane and tends to assume smallest possible area due to
questions.questionHtml: Free surface of a liquid behaves like a stretched membrane and tends to assume smallest possible area due to
questions.solutionText: Solution The molecules of the liquid exert a molecular force on other molecules of the same substance around them which is known as cohesive force. For a molecule inside the liquid, it is surrounded by the other molecules from every direction thus they experience a net zero resultant force due to the cohesive force. But for molecule on the top layer experiences force only from the molecule below it, thus it experiences a net inward force from liquid molecules inside the top layer liquid thus there exist a net force on the top layer due to which it always tries to move inwards thus tends to assume the smallest possible area thus this phenomenon exists due to cohesive force.
questions.solution: Solution The molecules of the liquid exert a molecular force on other molecules of the same substance around them which is known as cohesive force. For a molecule inside the liquid, it is surrounded by the other molecules from every direction thus they experience a net zero resultant force due to the cohesive force. But for molecule on the top layer experiences force only from the molecule below it, thus it experiences a net inward force from liquid molecules inside the top layer liquid thus there exist a net force on the top layer due to which it always tries to move inwards thus tends to assume the smallest possible area thus this phenomenon exists due to cohesive force.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

The molecules of the liquid exert a molecular force on other molecules of the same substance around them which is known as cohesive force.

For a molecule inside the liquid, it is surrounded by the other molecules from every direction thus they experience a net zero resultant force due to the cohesive force.

But for molecule on the top layer experiences force only from the molecule below it, thus it experiences a net inward force from liquid molecules inside the top layer liquid thus there exist a net force on the top layer due to which it always tries to move inwards thus tends to assume the smallest possible area thus this phenomenon exists due to cohesive force.

A thin bar magnet of length L and pole strength m is bent at its midpoint such that the two halves are perpendicular.

questions.solutionText: Solution Each half has length L 2 and same pole strength m . Magnetic moment of each half: M ′ = m ⋅ L 2 The two halves are perpendicular, so angle between their moments = 90 ° M n e t = M ′ 2 + M ′ 2 = 2 M ′ = 2 ⋅ m L 2 = m L 2 Answer: B) m L 2
questions.solution: Solution Each half has length L 2 and same pole strength m . Magnetic moment of each half: M ′ = m ⋅ L 2 The two halves are perpendicular, so angle between their moments = 90 ° M n e t = M ′ 2 + M ′ 2 = 2 M ′ = 2 ⋅ m L 2 = m L 2 Answer: B) m L 2
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Each half has length L2 and same pole strength m.

Magnetic moment of each half: M′=m⋅L2

The two halves are perpendicular, so angle between their moments =90°

Mnet=M′2+M′2=2M′=2⋅mL2=mL2

Answer: B) mL2

In steady state the heat current will be same in both rods ∴ H 1 = H 2 Thermal conductivity of rods are K 1 and K 2 . The temperature at end of both rods are θ 1 and θ 2 .Length of wire is l and area of cross-section is A . So we can write θ 1 - θ I K 1 A = θ - θ 2 I K 2 A ( θ = temperature of junction ) K 1 θ 1 - θ = K 2 θ - θ 2 θ = K 1 θ 1 + K 2 θ 2 K 1 + K 2 .

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

In the given question we have to calculate temperature at junction.Suppose the temperature at junction is θ. In steady state the heat current will be same in both rods

∴H1=H2

Thermal conductivity of rods are K1 and K2 . The temperature at end of both rods are θ1 and θ2.Length of wire is l and area of cross-section is A.

So we can write

θ1-θIK1A=θ-θ2IK2A (θ=temperature of junction )

K1θ1-θ=K2θ-θ2

θ=K1θ1+K2θ2K1+K2.

questions.solutionText: Solution V 1 = V L = 300 V, V 2 = V C = 300 V, source = 220 V, R = 100 Ω Since V L = V C = 300 V (equal and opposite in phase), they cancel: V 3 = V R = V s o u r c e 2 − ( V L − V C ) 2 = 220 2 − 0 = 220 V Current: I = V R R = 220 100 = 2.2 A Answer: Option (2) 220 V and 2.2 A
questions.solution: Solution V 1 = V L = 300 V, V 2 = V C = 300 V, source = 220 V, R = 100 Ω Since V L = V C = 300 V (equal and opposite in phase), they cancel: V 3 = V R = V s o u r c e 2 − ( V L − V C ) 2 = 220 2 − 0 = 220 V Current: I = V R R = 220 100 = 2.2 A Answer: Option (2) 220 V and 2.2 A
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
V1=VL=300 V, V2=VC=300 V, source =220 V, R=100 Ω

Since VL=VC=300 V (equal and opposite in phase), they cancel:
V3=VR=Vsource2−(VL−VC)2=2202−0=220 V

Current: I=VRR=220100=2.2 A

Answer: Option (2) 220 V and 2.2 A

questions.questionHtml: The resistance of 110M solution is 2.5×103ohm. What is the molar conductivity of solution? (cell constant =1.25cm-1 )
questions.solutionText: Solution cell constant, 1 a = 1.25 c m - 1 Resistance, R = 2.5 × 1 0 3 o h m We know that, R = ρ × 1 a ⇒ R = 1 κ × 1 a ⇒ κ = 5 × 1 0 - 4 c m - 1 o h m - 1 So, molar conductivity, λ m = κ × 1000 M = 5 × 1 0 - 4 × 1 0 3 1 10 = 5 d m - 3 c m 2 m o l - 1 = 5.0 d m - 1 c m 2 m o l - 1
questions.solution: Solution cell constant, 1 a = 1.25 c m - 1 Resistance, R = 2.5 × 1 0 3 o h m We know that, R = ρ × 1 a ⇒ R = 1 κ × 1 a ⇒ κ = 5 × 1 0 - 4 c m - 1 o h m - 1 So, molar conductivity, λ m = κ × 1000 M = 5 × 1 0 - 4 × 1 0 3 1 10 = 5 d m - 3 c m 2 m o l - 1 = 5.0 d m - 1 c m 2 m o l - 1
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
cell constant, 1a=1.25cm-1
Resistance, R=2.5×103ohm
We know that, R=ρ×1a
⇒R=1κ×1a
⇒κ=5×10-4cm-1ohm-1
So, molar conductivity, λm=κ×1000M=5×10-4×103110=5dm-3cm2mol-1=5.0dm-1cm2mol-1

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following species is not stable?
questions.questionText: Which of the following species is not stable?
questions.question: Which of the following species is not stable?
questions.prompt: Which of the following species is not stable?
questions.html: Which of the following species is not stable?
questions.questionHtml: Which of the following species is not stable?
questions.solutionText: Solution For an octahedral complex [ M X 6 ] 2 − , the central atom needs to have available d -orbitals to expand its octet and accommodate 6 ligands. [ S i F 6 ] 2 − : Si uses 3 d orbitals ⇒ stable. [ G e C l 6 ] 2 − : Ge uses 4 d orbitals ⇒ stable. [ S n ( O H ) 6 ] 2 − : Sn uses 4 d orbitals ⇒ stable. [ S i C l 6 ] 2 − : Si is in period 3 and does have 3 d orbitals, but C l is a large atom and 6 C l atoms cannot be accommodated around the small S i atom due to steric reasons ⇒ not stable. Answer: Option (4) [ S i C l 6 ] 2 −
questions.solution: Solution For an octahedral complex [ M X 6 ] 2 − , the central atom needs to have available d -orbitals to expand its octet and accommodate 6 ligands. [ S i F 6 ] 2 − : Si uses 3 d orbitals ⇒ stable. [ G e C l 6 ] 2 − : Ge uses 4 d orbitals ⇒ stable. [ S n ( O H ) 6 ] 2 − : Sn uses 4 d orbitals ⇒ stable. [ S i C l 6 ] 2 − : Si is in period 3 and does have 3 d orbitals, but C l is a large atom and 6 C l atoms cannot be accommodated around the small S i atom due to steric reasons ⇒ not stable. Answer: Option (4) [ S i C l 6 ] 2 −
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
For an octahedral complex [MX6]2−, the central atom needs to have available d-orbitals to expand its octet and accommodate 6 ligands.

[SiF6]2−: Si uses 3d orbitals ⇒ stable.
[GeCl6]2−: Ge uses 4d orbitals ⇒ stable.
[Sn(OH)6]2−: Sn uses 4d orbitals ⇒ stable.
[SiCl6]2−: Si is in period 3 and does have 3d orbitals, but Cl is a large atom and 6 Cl atoms cannot be accommodated around the small Si atom due to steric reasons ⇒ not stable.

Answer: Option (4) [SiCl6]2−

questions.solutionText: Solution Glyptal a polyester, is formed by ethylene glycol and phthalic acid formed by step growth polymerisation as shown in the above reaction. Hence, the correct option is (3).

questions.solutionText: Solution For an isobaric process, d S = d Q T = n C P d T T (taking n = 1 mol) Δ S = ∫ T 1 T 2 C P d T T = C P ln ⁡ T 2 T 1 = 2.303 C P log ⁡ T 2 T 1 Answer: Option (1)
questions.solution: Solution For an isobaric process, d S = d Q T = n C P d T T (taking n = 1 mol) Δ S = ∫ T 1 T 2 C P d T T = C P ln ⁡ T 2 T 1 = 2.303 C P log ⁡ T 2 T 1 Answer: Option (1)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
For an isobaric process, dS=dQT=nCPdTT (taking n=1 mol)

ΔS=∫T1T2CPdTT=CPln⁡T2T1=2.303CPlog⁡T2T1

Answer: Option (1)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: In the chlorination of benzene to form chlorobenzene, FeCl 3 is used as a Lewis acid catalyst. What is the primary role of FeCl 3 in this electrophilic substitution reaction?
questions.questionText: In the chlorination of benzene to form chlorobenzene, FeCl 3 is used as a Lewis acid catalyst. What is the primary role of FeCl 3 in this electrophilic substitution reaction?
questions.question: In the chlorination of benzene to form chlorobenzene, FeCl 3 is used as a Lewis acid catalyst. What is the primary role of FeCl 3 in this electrophilic substitution reaction?
questions.prompt: In the chlorination of benzene to form chlorobenzene, FeCl 3 is used as a Lewis acid catalyst. What is the primary role of FeCl 3 in this electrophilic substitution reaction?
questions.html: In the chlorination of benzene to form chlorobenzene, FeCl3 is used as a Lewis acid catalyst. What is the primary role of FeCl3 in this electrophilic substitution reaction?
questions.questionHtml: In the chlorination of benzene to form chlorobenzene, FeCl3 is used as a Lewis acid catalyst. What is the primary role of FeCl3 in this electrophilic substitution reaction?
questions.solutionText: Solution F e C l 3 acts as a Lewis acid by accepting a lone pair from C l 2 : C l 2 + F e C l 3 → C l + + F e C l 4 − The C l + (electrophile) then attacks the benzene ring in the electrophilic substitution reaction. Answer: B) To generate the electrophile C l + by reacting with C l 2 .
questions.solution: Solution F e C l 3 acts as a Lewis acid by accepting a lone pair from C l 2 : C l 2 + F e C l 3 → C l + + F e C l 4 − The C l + (electrophile) then attacks the benzene ring in the electrophilic substitution reaction. Answer: B) To generate the electrophile C l + by reacting with C l 2 .
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
FeCl3 acts as a Lewis acid by accepting a lone pair from Cl2:

Cl2+FeCl3→Cl++FeCl4−

The Cl+ (electrophile) then attacks the benzene ring in the electrophilic substitution reaction.

Answer: B) To generate the electrophile Cl+ by reacting with Cl2.

questions.questionHtml: A metal crystallises in a face centred cubic structure. If the edge length of the unit cell is 'a', the closest approach between the two atoms in the metallic crystal will be
questions.solutionText: Solution For face centred cubic structure, the closest approach between two atoms in metallic crystal will be equal to one half the length of the face diagonal. It will be 1 2 × length of face diagonal ...... 1 But the length of a face diagonal = 2 a ....... 2 Here, a is the edge of its unit cell. From 1 and 2 , the closest approach between two atoms in metallic crystal will be 2 R = 2 a 2 = a 2 .

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

For face centred cubic structure, the closest approach between two atoms in metallic crystal will be equal to one half the length of the face diagonal. It will be
12×length of face diagonal......1
But the length of a face diagonal =2a.......2
Here, a is the edge of its unit cell.
From 1 and 2, the closest approach between two atoms in metallic crystal will be
2R=2a2=a2.

questions.html: EMF of cell Ni|Ni2+(1.0M)‖Au3+(1.0M)|Au (Where E∘ for Ni2+∣Ni is −0.25V; E∘ for Au+3∣Au is 1.50 V ) is
questions.questionHtml: EMF of cell Ni|Ni2+(1.0M)‖Au3+(1.0M)|Au (Where E∘ for Ni2+∣Ni is −0.25V; E∘ for Au+3∣Au is 1.50 V ) is
questions.solutionText: Solution Cell: N i | N i 2 + | | A u 3 + | A u Anode (oxidation): N i → N i 2 + + 2 e − , E ° o x = + 0.25 V Cathode (reduction): A u 3 + + 3 e − → A u , E ° r e d = + 1.50 V E ° c e l l = E ° c a t h o d e + E ° a n o d e = 1.50 + 0.25 = 1.75 V Since concentrations are 1.0 M, no Nernst correction needed. Answer: Option (3) + 1.75 V
questions.solution: Solution Cell: N i | N i 2 + | | A u 3 + | A u Anode (oxidation): N i → N i 2 + + 2 e − , E ° o x = + 0.25 V Cathode (reduction): A u 3 + + 3 e − → A u , E ° r e d = + 1.50 V E ° c e l l = E ° c a t h o d e + E ° a n o d e = 1.50 + 0.25 = 1.75 V Since concentrations are 1.0 M, no Nernst correction needed. Answer: Option (3) + 1.75 V
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Cell: Ni | Ni2+ || Au3+ | Au

Anode (oxidation): Ni→Ni2++2e−, E°ox=+0.25 V
Cathode (reduction): Au3++3e−→Au, E°red=+1.50 V

E°cell=E°cathode+E°anode=1.50+0.25=1.75 V

Since concentrations are 1.0 M, no Nernst correction needed.

Answer: Option (3) +1.75 V

questions.solutionText: Solution 3 A g + 4 H N O 3 ( d i l . ) → Δ 3 A g N O 3 + N O ↑ + 2 H 2 O Dilute H N O 3 reacts with silver on heating to give silver nitrate, N O gas and water. questions.solution: Solution 3 A g + 4 H N O 3 ( d i l . ) → Δ 3 A g N O 3 + N O ↑ + 2 H 2 O Dilute H N O 3 reacts with silver on heating to give silver nitrate, N O gas and water. questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
3Ag+4HNO3 (dil.)→Δ3AgNO3+NO↑+2H2O

Dilute HNO3 reacts with silver on heating to give silver nitrate, NO gas and water. This is the correct method of preparation.

Conc. HNO3 gives NO2 instead and is not preferred. Aqua-regia dissolves Ag but does not give AgNO3.

Answer: Option (3)

questions.text: Which of the following polymer has strongest intermolecular forces of attraction?

questions.question: Which of the following polymer has strongest intermolecular forces of attraction?
questions.prompt: Which of the following polymer has strongest intermolecular forces of attraction?
questions.html: Which of the following polymer has strongest intermolecular forces of attraction?
questions.questionHtml: Which of the following polymer has strongest intermolecular forces of attraction?
questions.solutionText: Solution Neoprene have the weakest intermolecular forces as it is a elastomer. There are only few cross-links between the chains. Terylene have strong intermolecular forces like dipole-dipole interaction. It is a fibre type of polymer. Polyster and polythene are thermoplastic types of polymer. They have intermolecular forces, which are intermediate between those of elastomers and fibres.

They have intermolecular forces, which are intermediate between those of elastomers and fibres.

Polyster and polythene are thermoplastic types of polymer. They have intermolecular forces, which are intermediate between those of elastomers and fibres.

questions.subject: English & Logical Reasoning
questions.text: Paragraph Scientists long believed that two nerve clusters in the human hypothalamus, called suprachiasmatic nuclei (SCNs), were what controlled our circadian rhythms. Those rhythms are the biological cycles that recur approximately every 24 hours in synchronization with the cycle of sunlight and darkness caused by Earth's rotation. Studies have demonstrated that in some animals, the SCNs control daily fluctuations in blood pressure, body temperature, activity level, and alertness, as well as the nighttime release of the sleep-promoting agent melatonin. Furthermore, cells in the human retina dedicated to transmitting information about light levels to the SCNs have recently been discovered. Four critical genes governing circadian cycles have been found to be active in every tissue, however, not just the SCNs, of flies, mice, and humans. In addition, when laboratory rats that usually ate at will were fed only once a day, peak activity of a clock gene in their livers shifted by 12 hours, whereas the same clock gene in the SCNs remained synchronized with light cycles. While scientists do not dispute the role of the SCNs in controlling core functions such as the regulation of body temperature and blood pressure, scientists now believe that circadian clocks in other organs and tissues may respond to external cues other than light-including temperature changes-that recur regularly every 24 hours. Question The passage mentions each of the following as a function regulated by the SCNs in some animals EXCEPT

questions.question: Paragraph Scientists long believed that two nerve clusters in the human hypothalamus, called suprachiasmatic nuclei (SCNs), were what controlled our circadian rhythms. Those rhythms are the biological cycles that recur approximately every 24 hours in synchronization with the cycle of sunlight and darkness caused by Earth's rotation. Studies have demonstrated that in some animals, the SCNs control daily fluctuations in blood pressure, body temperature, activity level, and alertness, as well as the nighttime release of the sleep-promoting agent melatonin. Furthermore, cells in the human retina dedicated to transmitting information about light levels to the SCNs have recently been discovered. Four critical genes governing circadian cycles have been found to be active in every tissue, however, not just the SCNs, of flies, mice, and humans. In addition, when laboratory rats that usually ate at will were fed only once a day, peak activity of a clock gene in their livers shifted by 12 hours, whereas the same clock gene in the SCNs remained synchronized with light cycles. While scientists do not dispute the role of the SCNs in controlling core functions such as the regulation of body temperature and blood pressure, scientists now believe that circadian clocks in other organs and tissues may respond to external cues other than light-including temperature changes-that recur regularly every 24 hours. Question The passage mentions each of the following as a function regulated by the SCNs in some animals EXCEPT

questions.solutionText: Solution This question asks about what is NOT specifically mentioned in the passage with regard to functions regulated by the SCN. Those functions, as identified in the passage, are blood pressure, body temperature, activity level, alertness, and the release of melatonin. A The passage includes activity level in its list of functions regulated by the SCN. B The passage includes blood pressure in its list of functions regulated by the SCN. C The passage includes alertness in its list of functions regulated by the SCN. D Correct. While the passage does say that cells in the human retina transmit information to the SCN, there is no suggestion that the SCN reciprocally control vision.

questions.solutionText: Solution According to the Given information, Given a series and we need to find next term of this given series, So, the series is, 2 , 3 , 18 , 115 , 854 , ? Now we see the pattern used in this series is, multiply and add of the square are the same number. 3 = 2 × 1 + 1 2 18 = 3 × 3 + 3 2 115 = 18 × 5 + 5 2 854 = 115 × 7 + 7 2 854 × 9 + 9 2 = 7686 + 81 = 7767 So, the next number of this series is, 7767 . Hence, the answer is 7767 .

questions.text: A hiker starts from Point A and walks 8 km North. He then turns Right and walks 6 km. After this, he turns Right again and walks 3 k m . Finally, he turns to his Left and walks 6 k m to reach Point B. What is the shortest distance between Point A and Point B ?
questions.questionText: A hiker starts from Point A and walks 8 km North. He then turns Right and walks 6 km. After this, he turns Right again and walks 3 k m . Finally, he turns to his Left and walks 6 k m to reach Point B. What is the shortest distance between Point A and Point B ?
questions.question: A hiker starts from Point A and walks 8 km North. He then turns Right and walks 6 km. After this, he turns Right again and walks 3 k m . Finally, he turns to his Left and walks 6 k m to reach Point B. What is the shortest distance between Point A and Point B ?
questions.prompt: A hiker starts from Point A and walks 8 km North. He then turns Right and walks 6 km. After this, he turns Right again and walks 3 k m . Finally, he turns to his Left and walks 6 k m to reach Point B. What is the shortest distance between Point A and Point B ?
questions.html:
A hiker starts from Point A and walks 8 km North. He then turns Right and walks 6 km. After this, he turns Right again and walks 3 km. Finally, he turns to his Left and walks 6 km to reach Point B.

What is the shortest distance between Point A and Point B ?
questions.questionHtml:
A hiker starts from Point A and walks 8 km North. He then turns Right and walks 6 km. After this, he turns Right again and walks 3 km. Finally, he turns to his Left and walks 6 km to reach Point B.

What is the shortest distance between Point A and Point B ?
questions.solutionText: Solution Tracing the path (taking North as + y , East as + x ): Start at A = ( 0 , 0 ) 8 km North → ( 0 , 8 ) Turn Right (now facing East), 6 km → ( 6 , 8 ) Turn Right (now facing South), 3 km → ( 6 , 5 ) Turn Left (now facing East), 6 km → ( 12 , 5 ) So B = ( 12 , 5 ) Shortest distance = 12 2 + 5 2 = 144 + 25 = 169 = 13 km Answer: (1) 13 km
questions.solution: Solution Tracing the path (taking North as + y , East as + x ): Start at A = ( 0 , 0 ) 8 km North → ( 0 , 8 ) Turn Right (now facing East), 6 km → ( 6 , 8 ) Turn Right (now facing South), 3 km → ( 6 , 5 ) Turn Left (now facing East), 6 km → ( 12 , 5 ) So B = ( 12 , 5 ) Shortest distance = 12 2 + 5 2 = 144 + 25 = 169 = 13 km Answer: (1) 13 km
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Tracing the path (taking North as +y, East as +x):

Start at A=(0,0)
8 km North →(0,8)
Turn Right (now facing East), 6 km →(6,8)
Turn Right (now facing South), 3 km →(6,5)
Turn Left (now facing East), 6 km →(12,5)

So B=(12,5)

Shortest distance =122+52=144+25=169=13 km

Answer: (1) 13 km

questions.solutionText: Solution The member is elected by the people of that particular constituency and represents those people in the legislative assembly and debates on issues related to his or her constituency. The MLA's position is like an MP, but the difference is only that MLA is at the state level and the MP is at the national level. All MPs and MLAs are in the elected house.
questions.solution: Solution The member is elected by the people of that particular constituency and represents those people in the legislative assembly and debates on issues related to his or her constituency. The MLA's position is like an MP, but the difference is only that MLA is at the state level and the MP is at the national level. All MPs and MLAs are in the elected house.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

The member is elected by the people of that particular constituency and represents those people in the legislative assembly and debates on issues related to his or her constituency. The MLA's position is like an MP, but the difference is only that MLA is at the state level and the MP is at the national level. All MPs and MLAs are in the elected house.

questions.subject: English & Logical Reasoning
questions.text: In a certain code language, STADIUM is written as BUTDNVJ. How is JOURNEY written in that same code?
questions.questionText: In a certain code language, STADIUM is written as BUTDNVJ. How is JOURNEY written in that same code?
questions.question: In a certain code language, STADIUM is written as BUTDNVJ. How is JOURNEY written in that same code?
questions.prompt: In a certain code language, STADIUM is written as BUTDNVJ. How is JOURNEY written in that same code?
questions.html: In a certain code language, STADIUM is written as BUTDNVJ. How is JOURNEY written in that same code?
questions.questionHtml: In a certain code language, STADIUM is written as BUTDNVJ. How is JOURNEY written in that same code?
questions.solutionText: Solution Decoding Rule: Keep the middle letter same, reverse the 3 letters on each side, then add +1 to each of those letters. STADIUM: S,T,A | D | I,U,M Left reversed: A,T,S → +1 → B,U,T Middle: D → D Right reversed: M,U,I → +1 → N,V,J Result: B,U,T,D,N,V,J → BUTDNVJ. JOURNEY: J,O,U | R | N,E,Y Left reversed: U,O,J → +1 → V,P,K Middle: R → R Right reversed: Y,E,N → +1 → Z,F,O Result: V,P,K,R,Z,F,O → VPKRZFO Answer: Option (1) VPKRZFO
questions.solution: Solution Decoding Rule: Keep the middle letter same, reverse the 3 letters on each side, then add +1 to each of those letters. STADIUM: S,T,A | D | I,U,M Left reversed: A,T,S → +1 → B,U,T Middle: D → D Right reversed: M,U,I → +1 → N,V,J Result: B,U,T,D,N,V,J → BUTDNVJ. JOURNEY: J,O,U | R | N,E,Y Left reversed: U,O,J → +1 → V,P,K Middle: R → R Right reversed: Y,E,N → +1 → Z,F,O Result: V,P,K,R,Z,F,O → VPKRZFO Answer: Option (1) VPKRZFO
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Decoding Rule: Keep the middle letter same, reverse the 3 letters on each side, then add +1 to each of those letters.

STADIUM: S,T,A | D | I,U,M
Left reversed: A,T,S → +1 → B,U,T
Middle: D → D
Right reversed: M,U,I → +1 → N,V,J
Result: B,U,T,D,N,V,J → BUTDNVJ.

JOURNEY: J,O,U | R | N,E,Y
Left reversed: U,O,J → +1 → V,P,K
Middle: R → R
Right reversed: Y,E,N → +1 → Z,F,O
Result: V,P,K,R,Z,F,O → VPKRZFO

Answer: Option (1) VPKRZFO

questions.questionHtml:
Choose the pair that best expresses a relationship similar to that expressed in the original pair.

TENET : THEOLOGIAN :: ____
questions.solutionText: Solution A TENET is a core belief or principle that a THEOLOGIAN formally studies and builds their discipline around. The relationship is: foundational principle : the expert whose discipline is built on it. (1) Doctrine : Cleric — a cleric follows doctrine but does not build a scientific discipline around it. (2) Axiom : Mathematician — an axiom is a starting assumption, not a studied belief system; (3) Theory : Physicist — a theory is the foundational framework that a physicist formulates, tests, and builds their discipline upon. This best mirrors the relationship. (4) Creed : Disciple — a disciple follows a creed but is not a professional expert. Answer: (3) Theory : Physicist
questions.solution: Solution A TENET is a core belief or principle that a THEOLOGIAN formally studies and builds their discipline around. The relationship is: foundational principle : the expert whose discipline is built on it. (1) Doctrine : Cleric — a cleric follows doctrine but does not build a scientific discipline around it. (2) Axiom : Mathematician — an axiom is a starting assumption, not a studied belief system; (3) Theory : Physicist — a theory is the foundational framework that a physicist formulates, tests, and builds their discipline upon. This best mirrors the relationship. (4) Creed : Disciple — a disciple follows a creed but is not a professional expert. Answer: (3) Theory : Physicist
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
A TENET is a core belief or principle that a THEOLOGIAN formally studies and builds their discipline around.

The relationship is: foundational principle : the expert whose discipline is built on it.

(1) Doctrine : Cleric — a cleric follows doctrine but does not build a scientific discipline around it.
(2) Axiom : Mathematician — an axiom is a starting assumption, not a studied belief system;
(3) Theory : Physicist — a theory is the foundational framework that a physicist formulates, tests, and builds their discipline upon. This best mirrors the relationship.
(4) Creed : Disciple — a disciple follows a creed but is not a professional expert.

Answer: (3) Theory : Physicist

questions.subject: English & Logical Reasoning
questions.text: Find the next term in the series: 13 , 16 , 23 , 40 , 92 , 251 , ?
questions.questionText: Find the next term in the series: 13 , 16 , 23 , 40 , 92 , 251 , ?
questions.question: Find the next term in the series: 13 , 16 , 23 , 40 , 92 , 251 , ?
questions.prompt: Find the next term in the series: 13 , 16 , 23 , 40 , 92 , 251 , ?
questions.html:
Find the next term in the series:
13,16,23,40,92,251,?
questions.questionHtml:
Find the next term in the series:
13,16,23,40,92,251,?
questions.solutionText: Solution We begin by finding the differences between consecutive terms: Term Position Term Difference 1 13 − 2 16 16 − 13 = 3 3 23 23 − 16 = 7 4 40 40 − 23 = 17 5 92 92 − 40 = 52 6 251 251 − 92 = 159 Now observe the pattern in the differences: 3 , 7 , 17 , 52 , 159 Let's analyze how these differences are formed: - 7 = 3 × 2 + 1 - 17 = 7 × 2 + 3 - 52 = 17 × 3 + 1 - 159 = 52 × 3 + 3 We observe an alternating pattern in multipliers and additions: - Multiply by: 2, 2, 3, 3, ... - Add: 1, 3, 1, 3, ... So, the next difference should be: - 159 × 4 + 1 = 636 + 1 = 637 Now, add this to the last term: - 251 + 637 = 888
questions.solution: Solution We begin by finding the differences between consecutive terms: Term Position Term Difference 1 13 − 2 16 16 − 13 = 3 3 23 23 − 16 = 7 4 40 40 − 23 = 17 5 92 92 − 40 = 52 6 251 251 − 92 = 159 Now observe the pattern in the differences: 3 , 7 , 17 , 52 , 159 Let's analyze how these differences are formed: - 7 = 3 × 2 + 1 - 17 = 7 × 2 + 3 - 52 = 17 × 3 + 1 - 159 = 52 × 3 + 3 We observe an alternating pattern in multipliers and additions: - Multiply by: 2, 2, 3, 3, ... - Add: 1, 3, 1, 3, ... So, the next difference should be: - 159 × 4 + 1 = 636 + 1 = 637 Now, add this to the last term: - 251 + 637 = 888
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
We begin by finding the differences between consecutive terms:
Term PositionTermDifference113−21616−13=332323−16=744040−23=1759292−40=526251251−92=159
Now observe the pattern in the differences:
3,7,17,52,159
Let's analyze how these differences are formed:
- 7=3×2+1
- 17=7×2+3
- 52=17×3+1
- 159=52×3+3
We observe an alternating pattern in multipliers and additions:
- Multiply by: 2, 2, 3, 3, ...
- Add: 1, 3, 1, 3, ...
So, the next difference should be:
- 159×4+1=636+1=637
Now, add this to the last term:

questions.questionHtml:
If ,
'>' denotes ' + '
'<' denotes '−'
'+' denotes ' ÷ '
'−' denotes '='
'×' denotes '>'
'=' denotes '<'
Choose the correct statement from the following:
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Symbol mapping:
> ⇒+
< ⇒−
+ ⇒÷
− ⇒ =
× ⇒ >
= ⇒ <

Decode each option:

(1) 14+7>3=6+3>2
→14÷7+3=6÷3+2
→2+3=2+2→5=4. False.

(2) 6+3>8=4+2<1
→6÷3+8=4÷2−1
→2+8=2−1→10=1. False.

(3) 13<6+3=7>4
→13−6÷3=7+4
→13−2=11⇒11=11. True.

(4) 4>6+2×32+4<1
→4+6÷2>32÷4−1
→4+3>8−1→7>7. False.

Answer: Option (3)

questions.text: 1. M % N means M is the son of N . 2. M @ N means M is the sister of N . 3. M $ N means M is the father of N . Which of the following shows the relation that C is the grand daughter of E ?
questions.questionText: 1. M % N means M is the son of N . 2. M @ N means M is the sister of N . 3. M $ N means M is the father of N . Which of the following shows the relation that C is the grand daughter of E ?
questions.question: 1. M % N means M is the son of N . 2. M @ N means M is the sister of N . 3. M $ N means M is the father of N . Which of the following shows the relation that C is the grand daughter of E ?
questions.prompt: 1. M % N means M is the son of N . 2. M @ N means M is the sister of N . 3. M $ N means M is the father of N . Which of the following shows the relation that C is the grand daughter of E ?
questions.html:
1. M%N means M is the son of N.
2. M@ N means M is the sister of N.
3. M$N means M is the father of N.
Which of the following shows the relation that C is the grand daughter of E ?
questions.questionHtml:
1. M%N means M is the son of N.
2. M@ N means M is the sister of N.
3. M$N means M is the father of N.
Which of the following shows the relation that C is the grand daughter of E ?
questions.solutionText: Solution C @ B → C is the sister of B B % F → B is the son of F Hence, → C is the daughter of F F % E → F is the son of E Hence, → C is the grand daughter of E .
questions.solution: Solution C @ B → C is the sister of B B % F → B is the son of F Hence, → C is the daughter of F F % E → F is the son of E Hence, → C is the grand daughter of E .
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
C@B→C is the sister of B
B%F→B is the son of F
Hence, →C is the daughter of F
F%E→F is the son of E
Hence, →C is the grand daughter of E.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.text: If sin ⁡ θ + sin ⁡ ϕ = a and cos ⁡ θ + cos ⁡ ϕ = b , then tan ⁡ θ − ϕ 2 is equal to
questions.questionText: If sin ⁡ θ + sin ⁡ ϕ = a and cos ⁡ θ + cos ⁡ ϕ = b , then tan ⁡ θ − ϕ 2 is equal to
questions.question: If sin ⁡ θ + sin ⁡ ϕ = a and cos ⁡ θ + cos ⁡ ϕ = b , then tan ⁡ θ − ϕ 2 is equal to
questions.prompt: If sin ⁡ θ + sin ⁡ ϕ = a and cos ⁡ θ + cos ⁡ ϕ = b , then tan ⁡ θ − ϕ 2 is equal to
questions.html: If sin⁡θ+sin⁡ϕ=a and cos⁡θ+cos⁡ϕ=b, then tan⁡θ−ϕ2 is equal to
questions.questionHtml: If sin⁡θ+sin⁡ϕ=a and cos⁡θ+cos⁡ϕ=b, then tan⁡θ−ϕ2 is equal to
questions.solutionText: Solution Given that sin ⁡ θ + sin ⁡ ϕ = a and cos ⁡ θ + cos ⁡ ϕ = b Squaring sin 2 ⁡ θ + sin 2 ⁡ ϕ + 2 sin ⁡ θ sin ⁡ ϕ Squaring, sin 2 ⁡ θ + sin 2 ⁡ ϕ + 2 sin ⁡ θ sin ⁡ ϕ = a 2 and cos 2 ⁡ θ + cos 2 ⁡ ϕ + 2 cos ⁡ θ cos ⁡ ϕ = b 2 Adding, 2 + 2 ( sin ⁡ θ sin ⁡ ϕ + cos ⁡ θ cos ⁡ ϕ ) = a 2 + b 2 ⇒ 2 cos ⁡ ( θ − ϕ ) = a 2 + b 2 − 2 ⇒ cos ⁡ ( θ − ϕ ) = a 2 + b 2 − 2 2 ⇒ 1 − tan 2 ⁡ θ − ϕ 2 1 + tan 2 ⁡ θ − ϕ 2 = a 2 + b 2 − 2 2 ⇒ ( a 2 + b 2 ) + ( a 2 + b 2 ) tan 2 ⁡ θ − ϕ 2 − 2 − 2 tan 2 ⁡ θ − ϕ 2 = 2 − 2 tan 2 ⁡ θ − ϕ 2 ⇒ 4 − a 2 − b 2 a 2 + b 2 = tan 2 ⁡ θ − ϕ 2 ⇒ tan ⁡ ( θ − ϕ ) 2 = 4 − a 2 − b 2 a 2 + b 2 Trick : Put θ = π 2 , ϕ = 0 ∘ , then a = 1 = b ∴ tan ⁡ θ − ϕ 2 = 1 , which is given by (a) and (b). Again putting θ = π 4 = ϕ , we get tan ⁡ θ − ϕ 2 = 0 , which is given by (b).
questions.solution: Solution Given that sin ⁡ θ + sin ⁡ ϕ = a and cos ⁡ θ + cos ⁡ ϕ = b Squaring sin 2 ⁡ θ + sin 2 ⁡ ϕ + 2 sin ⁡ θ sin ⁡ ϕ Squaring, sin 2 ⁡ θ + sin 2 ⁡ ϕ + 2 sin ⁡ θ sin ⁡ ϕ = a 2 and cos 2 ⁡ θ + cos 2 ⁡ ϕ + 2 cos ⁡ θ cos ⁡ ϕ = b 2 Adding, 2 + 2 ( sin ⁡ θ sin ⁡ ϕ + cos ⁡ θ cos ⁡ ϕ ) = a 2 + b 2 ⇒ 2 cos ⁡ ( θ − ϕ ) = a 2 + b 2 − 2 ⇒ cos ⁡ ( θ − ϕ ) = a 2 + b 2 − 2 2 ⇒ 1 − tan 2 ⁡ θ − ϕ 2 1 + tan 2 ⁡ θ − ϕ 2 = a 2 + b 2 − 2 2 ⇒ ( a 2 + b 2 ) + ( a 2 + b 2 ) tan 2 ⁡ θ − ϕ 2 − 2 − 2 tan 2 ⁡ θ − ϕ 2 = 2 − 2 tan 2 ⁡ θ − ϕ 2 ⇒ 4 − a 2 − b 2 a 2 + b 2 = tan 2 ⁡ θ − ϕ 2 ⇒ tan ⁡ ( θ − ϕ ) 2 = 4 − a 2 − b 2 a 2 + b 2 Trick : Put θ = π 2 , ϕ = 0 ∘ , then a = 1 = b ∴ tan ⁡ θ − ϕ 2 = 1 , which is given by (a) and (b). Again putting θ = π 4 = ϕ , we get tan ⁡ θ − ϕ 2 = 0 , which is given by (b).
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Given that sin⁡θ+sin⁡ϕ=a
and cos⁡θ+cos⁡ϕ=b
Squaring sin2⁡θ+sin2⁡ϕ+2sin⁡θsin⁡ϕ
Squaring, sin2⁡θ+sin2⁡ϕ+2sin⁡θsin⁡ϕ=a2 and cos2⁡θ+cos2⁡ϕ+2cos⁡θcos⁡ϕ=b2
Adding, 2+2(sin⁡θsin⁡ϕ+cos⁡θcos⁡ϕ)=a2+b2
⇒2cos⁡(θ−ϕ)=a2+b2−2⇒
cos⁡(θ−ϕ)=a2+b2−22
⇒1−tan2⁡θ−ϕ21+tan2⁡θ−ϕ2=a2+b2−22
⇒(a2+b2)+(a2+b2)tan2⁡θ−ϕ2−2−2tan2⁡θ−ϕ2
=2−2tan2⁡θ−ϕ2
⇒4−a2−b2a2+b2=tan2⁡θ−ϕ2⇒
tan⁡(θ−ϕ)2=4−a2−b2a2+b2
Trick : Put θ=π2,ϕ=0∘, then a=1=b
∴tan⁡θ−ϕ2=1, which is given by (a) and (b).
Again putting θ=π4=ϕ, we get tan⁡θ−ϕ2=0, which is given by (b).

questions.solutionText: Solution If each observation is multiplied with p & then q is subtracted New mean x - 1 = p x - - q ⇒ 10 = p 20 - q . . . . . 1 and new standard deviations σ 2 = p σ 1 ⇒ 1 = p 2 ⇒ p = 1 2 ⇒ p = ± 1 2 If p = 1 2 then q = 0 (from equation (1)) If p = - 1 2 q = - 20

questions.solutionText: Solution T r + 1 = ( 9 r ) ( x 2 ) 9 − r ( − 1 3 x ) r = ( 9 r ) ( − 1 ) r 3 r ⋅ x 18 − 3 r For term independent of x : 18 − 3 r = 0 ⇒ r = 6 T 7 = ( 9 6 ) ⋅ ( − 1 ) 6 3 6 = ( 9 6 ) ⋅ 1 729 Written as k ⋅ ( 9 6 ) : k = 1 729 Answer: Option (2)
questions.solution: Solution T r + 1 = ( 9 r ) ( x 2 ) 9 − r ( − 1 3 x ) r = ( 9 r ) ( − 1 ) r 3 r ⋅ x 18 − 3 r For term independent of x : 18 − 3 r = 0 ⇒ r = 6 T 7 = ( 9 6 ) ⋅ ( − 1 ) 6 3 6 = ( 9 6 ) ⋅ 1 729 Written as k ⋅ ( 9 6 ) : k = 1 729 Answer: Option (2)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Tr+1=(9r)(x2)9−r(−13x)r=(9r)(−1)r3r⋅x18−3r

For term independent of x:
18−3r=0⇒r=6

T7=(96)⋅(−1)636=(96)⋅1729

For minimum of px + qy,
By AM ≥ GM
px+qy2≥px×qy

questions.solutionText: Solution x 2 + y 2 + a x + 2 a y + c = 0 2 g 2 - c = 2 a 2 4 - c = 2 2 ⇒ a 2 4 - c = 2 . . . 1 2 f 2 - c = 2 a 2 - c = 2 5 ⇒ a 2 - c = 5 . . . 2 From equation 1 & 2 we get 3 a 2 4 = 3 ⇒ a = - 2 a < 0 ∴ c = - 1 Circle ⇒ x 2 + y 2 - 2 x - 4 y - 1 = 0 ⇒ x - 1 2 + y - 2 2 = 6 Given x + 2 y = 0 ⇒ m = - 1 2 m tangent = 2 Equation of tangent ⇒ y - 2 = 2 x - 1 ± 6 1 + 4 ⇒ 2 x - y ± 30 = 0 Perpendicular distance from 0 , 0 = ± 30 4 + 1 = 6
questions.solution: Solution x 2 + y 2 + a x + 2 a y + c = 0 2 g 2 - c = 2 a 2 4 - c = 2 2 ⇒ a 2 4 - c = 2 . . . 1 2 f 2 - c = 2 a 2 - c = 2 5 ⇒ a 2 - c = 5 . . . 2 From equation 1 & 2 we get 3 a 2 4 = 3 ⇒ a = - 2 a < 0 ∴ c = - 1 Circle ⇒ x 2 + y 2 - 2 x - 4 y - 1 = 0 ⇒ x - 1 2 + y - 2 2 = 6 Given x + 2 y = 0 ⇒ m = - 1 2 m tangent = 2 Equation of tangent ⇒ y - 2 = 2 x - 1 ± 6 1 + 4 ⇒ 2 x - y ± 30 = 0 Perpendicular distance from 0 , 0 = ± 30 4 + 1 = 6
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
x2+y2+ax+2ay+c=0

2g2-c=2a24-c=22

⇒ a24-c=2 ...1

2f2-c=2a2-c=25

⇒a2-c=5 ...2

From equation 1 & 2 we get

3a24=3⇒a=-2 a<0

∴ c=-1

Circle ⇒x2+y2-2x-4y-1=0

⇒x-12+y-22=6

Given x+2y=0⇒m=-12

mtangent=2

Equation of tangent ⇒y-2=2x-1±61+4

⇒2x-y±30=0

Perpendicular distance from 0,0=±304+1=6

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.text: The sum of an infinite geometric progression is 57 and the sum of their cubes is 9747 , then what will be the common ratio of the geometric progression?
questions.questionText: The sum of an infinite geometric progression is 57 and the sum of their cubes is 9747 , then what will be the common ratio of the geometric progression?
questions.question: The sum of an infinite geometric progression is 57 and the sum of their cubes is 9747 , then what will be the common ratio of the geometric progression?
questions.prompt: The sum of an infinite geometric progression is 57 and the sum of their cubes is 9747 , then what will be the common ratio of the geometric progression?
questions.html: The sum of an infinite geometric progression is 57 and the sum of their cubes is 9747, then what will be the common ratio of the geometric progression?
questions.questionHtml: The sum of an infinite geometric progression is 57 and the sum of their cubes is 9747, then what will be the common ratio of the geometric progression?
questions.solutionText: Solution Let a be the first term and r the common ratio of the G . P . Then sum = 57 ⇒ a 1 - r = 57 .......(i) Sum of the cubes = 9747 ⇒ a 3 + a 3 r 3 + a 3 r 6 + … = 9747 ⇒ a 3 1 - r 3 = 9747 .......(ii) Dividing the cube of (i) by (ii), we get a 3 ( 1 - r ) 3 · 1 - r 3 a 3 = ( 57 ) 3 9747 ⇒ 1 - r 3 ( 1 - r ) 3 = 19 ⇒ 1 + r + r 2 ( 1 - r ) 2 = 19 ⇒ 18 r 2 - 39 r + 18 = 0 ⇒ ( 3 r - 2 ) ( 6 r - 9 ) = 0 ⇒ r = 2 3 o r r = 3 2 ⇒ r = 2 3 ( ∵ r ≠ 3 2 because - 1 < r < 1 for an infinite G . P )
questions.solution: Solution Let a be the first term and r the common ratio of the G . P . Then sum = 57 ⇒ a 1 - r = 57 .......(i) Sum of the cubes = 9747 ⇒ a 3 + a 3 r 3 + a 3 r 6 + … = 9747 ⇒ a 3 1 - r 3 = 9747 .......(ii) Dividing the cube of (i) by (ii), we get a 3 ( 1 - r ) 3 · 1 - r 3 a 3 = ( 57 ) 3 9747 ⇒ 1 - r 3 ( 1 - r ) 3 = 19 ⇒ 1 + r + r 2 ( 1 - r ) 2 = 19 ⇒ 18 r 2 - 39 r + 18 = 0 ⇒ ( 3 r - 2 ) ( 6 r - 9 ) = 0 ⇒ r = 2 3 o r r = 3 2 ⇒ r = 2 3 ( ∵ r ≠ 3 2 because - 1 < r < 1 for an infinite G . P )
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

Let a be the first term and r the common ratio of the G.P. Then

sum=57⇒a1-r=57 .......(i)

Sum of the cubes =9747

⇒a3+a3r3+a3r6+…=9747

⇒a31-r3=9747 .......(ii)

Dividing the cube of (i) by (ii), we

get a3(1-r)3·1-r3a3=(57)39747

⇒1-r3(1-r)3=19

⇒1+r+r2(1-r)2=19

⇒18r2-39r+18=0⇒(3r-2)(6r-9)=0⇒r=23 or r=32⇒r=23

(∵ r≠32 because -1<r<1 for an infinite G.P)

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
For n×n matrix: |adj M|=|M|n−1, |M−1|=1|M|, |kM|=kn|M|

Here n=3, |A|=3, |B|=2.

|A−1 adjB−1 adj(3A−1)|

=|A−1|⋅|adj B−1|⋅|adj(3A−1)|

|A−1|=13

|B−1|=12⇒|adj B−1|=|B−1|n−1=(12)2=14

|3A−1|=33⋅|A−1|=27⋅13=9⇒|adj(3A−1)|=92=81

=13⋅14⋅81=8112=274

Answer: Option (2) 274

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.text: There are fifty persons among whom 2 are brothers. The number of ways they can be arranged in a circle, if there is exactly one person between the two brothers, is
questions.questionText: There are fifty persons among whom 2 are brothers. The number of ways they can be arranged in a circle, if there is exactly one person between the two brothers, is
questions.question: There are fifty persons among whom 2 are brothers. The number of ways they can be arranged in a circle, if there is exactly one person between the two brothers, is
questions.prompt: There are fifty persons among whom 2 are brothers. The number of ways they can be arranged in a circle, if there is exactly one person between the two brothers, is
questions.html: There are fifty persons among whom 2 are brothers. The number of ways they can be arranged in a circle, if there is exactly one person between the two brothers, is
questions.questionHtml: There are fifty persons among whom 2 are brothers. The number of ways they can be arranged in a circle, if there is exactly one person between the two brothers, is
questions.solutionText: Solution Fix the two brothers and the one person between them as a block. Choose the person between the two brothers: 48 ways (any of the remaining 48 people). The two brothers can swap positions (brother on left or right of the middle person): 2 ways. Now treat this block of 3 as a single unit. Total units in circle = 48 (block + 47 others). Circular arrangements of 48 units = 47 ! Total = 48 × 2 × 47 ! = 2 × 48 ! Answer: Option (1) 2 × 48 !
questions.solution: Solution Fix the two brothers and the one person between them as a block. Choose the person between the two brothers: 48 ways (any of the remaining 48 people). The two brothers can swap positions (brother on left or right of the middle person): 2 ways. Now treat this block of 3 as a single unit. Total units in circle = 48 (block + 47 others). Circular arrangements of 48 units = 47 ! Total = 48 × 2 × 47 ! = 2 × 48 ! Answer: Option (1) 2 × 48 !
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Fix the two brothers and the one person between them as a block.

Choose the person between the two brothers: 48 ways (any of the remaining 48 people).

The two brothers can swap positions (brother on left or right of the middle person): 2 ways.

Now treat this block of 3 as a single unit. Total units in circle =48 (block + 47 others).

Circular arrangements of 48 units =47!

Total =48×2×47!=2×48!

Answer: Option (1) 2×48!

questions.text: A man of height 2 m walks at a uniform speed of 7 m / min away from a lamp post of height 9 m . The rate ( m / min ) at which the length of his shadow increases is
questions.questionText: A man of height 2 m walks at a uniform speed of 7 m / min away from a lamp post of height 9 m . The rate ( m / min ) at which the length of his shadow increases is
questions.question: A man of height 2 m walks at a uniform speed of 7 m / min away from a lamp post of height 9 m . The rate ( m / min ) at which the length of his shadow increases is
questions.prompt: A man of height 2 m walks at a uniform speed of 7 m / min away from a lamp post of height 9 m . The rate ( m / min ) at which the length of his shadow increases is
questions.html: A man of height 2 m walks at a uniform speed of 7 m/min away from a lamp post of height 9 m. The rate (m/min) at which the length of his shadow increases is
questions.questionHtml: A man of height 2 m walks at a uniform speed of 7 m/min away from a lamp post of height 9 m. The rate (m/min) at which the length of his shadow increases is
questions.solutionText: Solution Let A B be the lamp-post and P Q the man, C P be his shadow at time t . Let A P = ‘ x , P C = y , Also A B = 9 m , P Q = 2 m Now, △ C A B and △ C P Q are equiangular and hence similar. ∴ P C A C = P Q A B ⇒ y x + y = 2 9 ⇒ 9 y = 2 x + 2 y ⇒ 7 y = 2 x ⇒ x = 7 2 y ⇒ d x d t = 7 2 d y d t (differentiating w.r.t. t ) But d x d t = 7 m / min ∴ 7 = 7 2 d y d t ⇒ d y d t = 2 m / min ∴ Length of shadow is increases at 2 m / min .
questions.solution: Solution Let A B be the lamp-post and P Q the man, C P be his shadow at time t . Let A P = ‘ x , P C = y , Also A B = 9 m , P Q = 2 m Now, △ C A B and △ C P Q are equiangular and hence similar. ∴ P C A C = P Q A B ⇒ y x + y = 2 9 ⇒ 9 y = 2 x + 2 y ⇒ 7 y = 2 x ⇒ x = 7 2 y ⇒ d x d t = 7 2 d y d t (differentiating w.r.t. t ) But d x d t = 7 m / min ∴ 7 = 7 2 d y d t ⇒ d y d t = 2 m / min ∴ Length of shadow is increases at 2 m / min .
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Let AB be the lamp-post and PQ the man, CP be his shadow at time t.
Let AP=‘x,PC=y, Also AB=9 m,PQ=2 m
Now, △CAB and △CPQ are equiangular and hence similar.

∴PCAC=PQAB⇒yx+y=29⇒9y=2x+2y⇒7y=2x⇒x=72y⇒dxdt=72dydt (differentiating w.r.t. t) But dxdt=7 m/min∴7=72dydt⇒dydt=2 m/min
∴ Length of shadow is increases at 2 m/min.

questions.text: If sin - 1 x + sin - 1 y = 2 π 3 and cos - 1 x - cos - 1 y = π 3 .Then, ( x , y ) is equal to
questions.questionText: If sin - 1 x + sin - 1 y = 2 π 3 and cos - 1 x - cos - 1 y = π 3 .Then, ( x , y ) is equal to
questions.question: If sin - 1 x + sin - 1 y = 2 π 3 and cos - 1 x - cos - 1 y = π 3 .Then, ( x , y ) is equal to
questions.prompt: If sin - 1 x + sin - 1 y = 2 π 3 and cos - 1 x - cos - 1 y = π 3 .Then, ( x , y ) is equal to
questions.html: If sin-1x+sin-1y=2π3 and cos-1x-cos-1y=π3.Then, (x, y) is equal to
questions.questionHtml: If sin-1x+sin-1y=2π3 and cos-1x-cos-1y=π3.Then, (x, y) is equal to
questions.solutionText: Solution Given, sin - 1 x + sin - 1 y = 2 π 3 . . . ( i ) and cos - 1 x - cos - 1 y = π 3 . . . ( i i ) We know that sin - 1 x + cos - 1 x = π 2 Using the above concept, we can write ⇒ π 2 - sin - 1 x - π 2 - sin - 1 y = π 3 ⇒ - sin - 1 x + sin - 1 y = π 3 . . . ( i i i ) On adding equation i and i i i , we get sin - 1 y = π 2 ⇒ y = 1 On subtracting equation i from equation i i i , we get sin - 1 x = π 6 ⇒ x = 1 2 ∴ x, y = 1 2 , 1
questions.solution: Solution Given, sin - 1 x + sin - 1 y = 2 π 3 . . . ( i ) and cos - 1 x - cos - 1 y = π 3 . . . ( i i ) We know that sin - 1 x + cos - 1 x = π 2 Using the above concept, we can write ⇒ π 2 - sin - 1 x - π 2 - sin - 1 y = π 3 ⇒ - sin - 1 x + sin - 1 y = π 3 . . . ( i i i ) On adding equation i and i i i , we get sin - 1 y = π 2 ⇒ y = 1 On subtracting equation i from equation i i i , we get sin - 1 x = π 6 ⇒ x = 1 2 ∴ x, y = 1 2 , 1
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

Given,

sin-1x+sin-1y=2π3 ...(i)
and cos-1x-cos-1y=π3 ...(ii)

We know that sin-1x+cos-1x=π2

Using the above concept, we can write
⇒π2-sin-1x-π2-sin-1y=π3
⇒-sin-1x+sin-1y=π3 ...(iii)
On adding equation i and iii, we get
sin-1y=π2⇒y=1
On subtracting equation i from equation iii, we get
sin-1x=π6⇒x=12
∴x, y=12,1

questions.questionHtml: If 0<A<B<π, sin⁡A-sin⁡B=12 and cos⁡A-cos⁡B=32, then the value of A+B is equal to
questions.solutionText: Solution sin ⁡ A - sin ⁡ B 2 + cos ⁡ A - cos ⁡ B 2 = 1 2 + 3 2 = 2 ⇒ 2 - 2 sin ⁡ A sin ⁡ B + cos ⁡ A cos ⁡ B = 2 ⇒ c o s B - A = 0 ⇒ B - A = π 2 ⇒ B = A + π 2 sin ⁡ A - sin ⁡ B = 1 2 ⇒ sin ⁡ A - cos ⁡ A = 1 2 ⇒ sin ⁡ A . 1 2 - cos ⁡ A . 1 2 = 1 2 ⇒ s i n A - π 4 = s i n π 6 ⇒ A = π 6 + π 4 = 5 π 12 A + B = A + A + π 2 = 5 π 6 + π 2 = 4 π 3
questions.solution: Solution sin ⁡ A - sin ⁡ B 2 + cos ⁡ A - cos ⁡ B 2 = 1 2 + 3 2 = 2 ⇒ 2 - 2 sin ⁡ A sin ⁡ B + cos ⁡ A cos ⁡ B = 2 ⇒ c o s B - A = 0 ⇒ B - A = π 2 ⇒ B = A + π 2 sin ⁡ A - sin ⁡ B = 1 2 ⇒ sin ⁡ A - cos ⁡ A = 1 2 ⇒ sin ⁡ A . 1 2 - cos ⁡ A . 1 2 = 1 2 ⇒ s i n A - π 4 = s i n π 6 ⇒ A = π 6 + π 4 = 5 π 12 A + B = A + A + π 2 = 5 π 6 + π 2 = 4 π 3
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
sin⁡A-sin⁡B2+cos⁡A-cos⁡B2=12+32=2
⇒2-2sin⁡Asin⁡B+cos⁡Acos⁡B=2
⇒cosB-A=0⇒B-A=π2⇒B=A+π2
sin⁡A-sin⁡B=12⇒sin⁡A-cos⁡A=12
⇒sin⁡A.12-cos⁡A.12=12
⇒sinA-π4=sinπ6
⇒A=π6+π4=5π12
A+B=A+A+π2=5π6+π2=4π3

questions.solutionText: Solution lim x → 0 cos ⁡ ( sin ⁡ x ) − cos ⁡ x x 4 = lim x → 0 2 sin ⁡ ( x + sin ⁡ x 2 ) sin ⁡ ( x − sin ⁡ x 2 ) x 4 = 2 lim x → 0 [ sin ⁡ ( x + sin ⁡ x 2 ) ( x + sin ⁡ x 2 ) × sin ⁡ ( x − sin ⁡ x 2 ) ( x − sin ⁡ x 2 ) × ( x + sin ⁡ x 2 x ) ( x − sin ⁡ x 2 x 3 ) ] = 2 lim x → 0 [ sin ⁡ ( x + sin ⁡ x 2 ) x + sin ⁡ x 2 × sin ⁡ ( x − sin ⁡ x 2 ) x − sin ⁡ x 2 = lim x → 0 x − sin ⁡ x x 3 = lim x → 0 x − ( x − x 3 3 ! + x 5 5 ! − … ) x 3 = lim x → 0 ( 1 3 ! − x 2 5 ! + … ) = 1 6
questions.solution: Solution lim x → 0 cos ⁡ ( sin ⁡ x ) − cos ⁡ x x 4 = lim x → 0 2 sin ⁡ ( x + sin ⁡ x 2 ) sin ⁡ ( x − sin ⁡ x 2 ) x 4 = 2 lim x → 0 [ sin ⁡ ( x + sin ⁡ x 2 ) ( x + sin ⁡ x 2 ) × sin ⁡ ( x − sin ⁡ x 2 ) ( x − sin ⁡ x 2 ) × ( x + sin ⁡ x 2 x ) ( x − sin ⁡ x 2 x 3 ) ] = 2 lim x → 0 [ sin ⁡ ( x + sin ⁡ x 2 ) x + sin ⁡ x 2 × sin ⁡ ( x − sin ⁡ x 2 ) x − sin ⁡ x 2 = lim x → 0 x − sin ⁡ x x 3 = lim x → 0 x − ( x − x 3 3 ! + x 5 5 ! − … ) x 3 = lim x → 0 ( 1 3 ! − x 2 5 ! + … ) = 1 6
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
limx→0cos⁡(sin⁡x)−cos⁡xx4=limx→02sin⁡(x+sin⁡x2)sin⁡(x−sin⁡x2)x4=2limx→0[sin⁡(x+sin⁡x2)(x+sin⁡x2)×sin⁡(x−sin⁡x2)(x−sin⁡x2)×(x+sin⁡x2x)(x−sin⁡x2x3)]=2limx→0[sin⁡(x+sin⁡x2)x+sin⁡x2×sin⁡(x−sin⁡x2)x−sin⁡x2=limx→0x−sin⁡xx3=limx→0x−(x−x33!+x55!−…)x3=limx→0(13!−x25!+…)=16

questions.solutionText: Solution tan ⁡ θ = 1 2 ⇒ sin ⁡ θ = 1 5 , cos ⁡ θ = 2 5 sin ⁡ 2 θ = 2 sin ⁡ θ cos ⁡ θ = 2 ⋅ 1 5 ⋅ 2 5 = 4 5 cos ⁡ 2 θ = 1 − 2 sin 2 ⁡ θ = 1 − 2 5 = 3 5 sin ⁡ 4 θ = 2 sin ⁡ 2 θ cos ⁡ 2 θ = 2 ⋅ 4 5 ⋅ 3 5 = 24 25 Answer: C) 24 25
questions.solution: Solution tan ⁡ θ = 1 2 ⇒ sin ⁡ θ = 1 5 , cos ⁡ θ = 2 5 sin ⁡ 2 θ = 2 sin ⁡ θ cos ⁡ θ = 2 ⋅ 1 5 ⋅ 2 5 = 4 5 cos ⁡ 2 θ = 1 − 2 sin 2 ⁡ θ = 1 − 2 5 = 3 5 sin ⁡ 4 θ = 2 sin ⁡ 2 θ cos ⁡ 2 θ = 2 ⋅ 4 5 ⋅ 3 5 = 24 25 Answer: C) 24 25
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
tan⁡θ=12⇒sin⁡θ=15, cos⁡θ=25

sin⁡2θ=2sin⁡θcos⁡θ=2⋅15⋅25=45

cos⁡2θ=1−2sin2⁡θ=1−25=35

sin⁡4θ=2sin⁡2θcos⁡2θ=2⋅45⋅35=2425

Answer: C) 2425
